title: Continuous Model Retraining vs Regression Risk
problem_statement:
An e-commerce recommendation engine generates $45M annually in attributed revenue. The model must retrain on fresh behavioral data (clicks, purchases, cart additions) to capture evolving user preferences and seasonal trends.

However, each retraining cycle introduces a 12-18% probability of regression on key segments — for example, a retrained model might improve overall click-through rate by 3% while catastrophically degrading recommendations for the highest-spending 5% of users by 40%.

The current approach retrains weekly with a 2-day canary period, but this creates a 9-day lag in capturing trend shifts (e.g., viral products, breaking news effects). Daily retraining would capture trends within 3 days but increases regression incidents from 2/month to 8/month, each costing an average $180K in lost revenue during the detection-and-rollback window.

The model needs to continuously learn from fresh data without ever regressing on critical user segments.

ground_truth.ifr_baseline: The model continuously improves from new data without any regression risk or monitoring overhead
